narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 36, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your profile that stood out as risks when compared to others who passed. The strongest influence pushing toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is below the typical 5.53 average; students in lower deciles like yours historically struggle more on the bar. Next, your Year 1 decile of 4—again under the average of 5.71—reinforced this concern, signaling early academic challenges that often carry over. Your upper-middle family income also played a notable role in tipping the scales toward failure, as the model associates this bracket with slightly higher bar failure rates in the data, perhaps due to related pressures or preparation differences. While your LSAT and GPA were close to averages and your full-time status is the norm for most applicants, they weren't enough to offset these top concerns. In short, your mid-range deciles were the biggest red flags compared to students who typically pass, who cluster in higher rankings. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model's data-driven lens saw these as the dominant hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for upper-middle income students, or applying to schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories like yours more heavily. Building a stronger academic trajectory now could flip the script for future applications.
